result:
No compile errors. Build is green.

**Root cause:** `equal_obj` in `crates/lua-vm/src/vm.rs:820-824` compared two `LuaClosure` values with `std::ptr::eq(f1 as *const _, f2 as *const _)`. The references `f1` and `f2` are addresses of the function fields inside two distinct `LuaValue` enum instances (cloned to locals before the call), so the pointer comparison always returned false — even when both held the same closure. That made `f == f` evaluate to `false` for any Lua function, breaking the `assert(a[i] == x[i])` in `vararg.lua` line 8 when `a[i]` and `x[i]` both held the same function `f`.

**The fix:** In `vm.rs`, properly destructure `LuaClosure` and compare by `GcRef::ptr_eq` for `Lua`/`C` variants and `==` for the `LightC` registry index. Mirrored the same logic in `lua-types/src/value.rs` (PartialEq for LuaValue and `lua_key_eq` for table keys) via a new `closure_eq` helper, so function values used as table keys also work.

**New state:** I have not re-run the test program per the instructions ("Do NOT run the program at the end"); the mega-loop will re-test. `cargo build -q -p lua-cli` is green. Earlier intermediate runs showed function-identity `==` returning `false` even when both operands printed identical addresses, which the fix corrects.

tool_input.command:
cat <<'EOF' > /tmp/vararg_test1.lua
local function f (a, ...)
  local x = {n = select('#', ...), ...}
  print("n =", x.n, "x[1] =", x[1], "x[2] =", x[2], "x[3] =", x[3])
  for i = 1, x.n do
    print("i =", i, "a[i] =", a[i], "x[i] =", x[i])
    assert(a[i] == x[i])
  end
  return x.n
end

assert(f() == 0)
print("test 1 passed")
assert(f({1,2,3}, 1, 2, 3) == 3)
print("test 2 passed")
EOF
target/debug/lua-rs "$(cat /tmp/vararg_test1.lua)" 2>&1 | tail -30
